turns.speaker: tutor
turns.text: So we saw that we can use the present tense to refer to the future. For example, if we have some future context we can just use the present tense by itself. So we could say I am having breakfast with them tomorrow. Now in Spanish, you don’t say with them. You say with they. With they. So how would that be, first?

turns.speaker: tutor
turns.text: Good. And to have breakfast Desayunar, like in English breakfast,“break fast”. So, I’m having breakfast with them tomorrow:

turns.speaker: tutor
turns.text: Good. Desayuno con ellos mañana. Good. And if we don’t have a future context, like mañana or any other context showing us it’s the future, we can also use I am going to speak about the future. So you can say I am going to have breakfast with them. How is that?

turns.speaker: tutor
turns.text: Good. Voy a desayunar con ellos. Good. And if we have the future context, it doesn’t mean we have to say desayuno con ellos mañana. We could also say voy a desayunar con ellos mañana. The fact that we have future context doesn’t mean we can’t use voy anymore. So we’ve seen in that way we can use the present to talk about the future, but this doesn’t mean that we don’t have a future tense in Spanish. We do. Luckily, we don’t have to learn any new sounds to master the future tense in Spanish. The future tense in Spanish is connected with the past tense that we learned. So let’s make a of haber. Haber. This past of I have had breakfast or I have eaten, I have gone. Now it’s very irregular, recap of the forms of haber haber, and we said that a nice trick to remember the different forms of haber is just looking at the two vowel sounds inside the verb haber. So what are those two vowel sounds?

turns.speaker: tutor
turns.text: Good. Let’s start with / e /. What does he mean by itself?

turns.speaker: student
expected_answers: I have.? What’s the plural of, let’s

turns.speaker: tutor
turns.text: Good. I have spelled h-e. And what other person is connected with I I say?

turns.speaker: tutor
turns.text: Good. We. So we have he for I have and hemos for we have. What is ha by itself? H-a:

turns.speaker: tutor
turns.text: Good. So give me the other two versions now.

turns.speaker: tutor
turns.text: Good. They or you guys.

turns.speaker: tutor
turns.text: Good. So that's our set. Let’s practice a little bit some structures with haber just to recap. So what was to sleep? Dormant:

turns.speaker: tutor
turns.text: Good. So how would you say I have slept? Begin with I have:

turns.speaker: tutor
turns.text: Good. We have slept:

turns.speaker: tutor
turns.text: He has slept:

turns.speaker: tutor
turns.text: Good. How would you say to recommend?

turns.speaker: tutor
turns.text: Good. So how would you say They have recommended it, they have recommended it, and I remind. There’s no option of you that with these structures with haber, the it has to come before haber where to put it. So They have recommended it:

turns.speaker: tutor
turns.text: Lo han recomendado. Good. So these forms that we have, these forms of haber that we have – – are also the endings that we have for the future tense. So like I said, he, hemos, ha, has, han we will learn the future tense now without having to learn any new sounds. Now, the interesting thing about the future tense is that we take the verb in its “to” form, where it’s an – ar verb, - er verb, or – ir verb, doesn’t matter, and we put the ending at the end of the “to” form. And we stress the ending. We are effectively pushing the accent into the future. For example, we have comer, and what was I have from haber?

turns.speaker: tutor
turns.text: He h, just the sound / e, and that’s for I. We add that on to comer and we get comeré I will eat. Now we are pushing the accent into the future, which might just seem like a coincidence. But you can notice that it happens in other languages as well, this phenomenon. For example, in Greek part of the past tense is to pull the accent back one syllable, to pull the accent back into the past. And actually, this is so important in Greek that if there is not a syllable there to do it, you add one to pull the accent back into the past. So this is a phenomenon that you see in different languages. The accents representing somehow or reflecting somehow the concept of time and tenses. So here we have pushing the accent into the future: comeré. I will eat. You can give me the rest of the versions for the future of comer.

turns.speaker: tutor
turns.text: Good. Comeremos: We will eat.

turns.speaker: tutor
turns.text: Good. He will eat.

turns.speaker: tutor
turns.text: You (informal) will eat.

turns.speaker: tutor
turns.text: Good. They or you guys will eat. Good. What was to go? We said it was very irregular. We have voy and vamos and va, but the verb to go doesn’t look anything like them.

turns.speaker: tutor
turns.text: Ir. Ir in the future is regular. So we can follow these rules and get the future tenses of to go: I will go, he will go. So what is I will go?

turns.speaker: tutor
turns.text: Iré. And we will go:

turns.speaker: tutor
turns.text: Iremos. He will go:

turns.speaker: tutor
turns.text: Irá. They or you guys:

turns.speaker: tutor
turns.text: Irán. Like the country. They will go to Iran:

turns.speaker: tutor
turns.text: Irán a Irán. Good. You (informal) will go:

turns.speaker: tutor
turns.text: Good. And what if we wanted to make it a question? Will you go?:

turns.speaker: tutor
turns.text: Good.:

turns.speaker: tutor
turns.text: Good. What was to find or to encounter?

turns.speaker: tutor
turns.text: Encontrar. I will find:

turns.speaker: tutor
turns.text: Good. Encontraré. I will find it:

turns.speaker: tutor
turns.text: Good. Lo encontraré. We will find it:

turns.text: That was an interesting error, because I said we, so I made I plural with we, and your mind made the it plural, ah?

turns.speaker: tutor

turns.speaker: tutor
turns.text: Good. Lo encontraremos. He or she or you (formal) will find it:

turns.speaker: tutor
turns.text: Good. Lo encontrará. He will find them, or she will find them, or you (formal) will find them:

turns.speaker: tutor
turns.text: So you made both of them them or they.

turns.speaker: tutor

turns.speaker: tutor
turns.text: Good. Los encontrará. So we want to think about the two things separately, ah? Who is doing the verb and who are they doing it to. Who or what are they doing it to. We said that quedar means to remain, quedar, and to get the meaning of I stay we say I remain myself. How was that? I remain myself:

turns.speaker: tutor
turns.text: Me quedo. Good. How would you say I will stay, then? I will remain myself.

turns.speaker: tutor
turns.text: Good, but quedaré means I will remain and if you want the meaning of stay, you must, must use-

turns.speaker: tutor
turns.text: Yeah.

turns.speaker: tutor
turns.text: Me quedaré. I will remain myself or I will stay. I will stay here:

turns.speaker: tutor
turns.text: Good. What was to prepare?

turns.speaker: tutor
turns.text: Good. And I will prepare:

turns.speaker: tutor
turns.text: Good. So it’s very important to think out the whole verb, preparar, and then add on the - e, prepararé. Because a very common mistake that learners make is to say preparé. And they miss out the last ar because they don’t think out the whole verb first. So we must think out the whole verb first, preparar, and then add on the ending, prepararé. What was to wait or to hope?

turns.speaker: tutor
turns.text: Esperar. I will wait:

turns.speaker: tutor
turns.text: Good. What was to organize?

turns.speaker: tutor
turns.text: Good. He will organize:

turns.speaker: tutor
turns.text: Good. Organizará to worry about where the accent goes when we’re pronouncing the whole verb, organizará. On the end. Organizará. They will organize:

turns.speaker: tutor
turns.text: Good. Organizarán. Good. What was tired?

turns.speaker: tutor
turns.text: Good. Cansado. So how would you say I will be tired? I will be tired:

turns.speaker: tutor
turns.text: Good. Estaré cansada. Good. How would it will be be, from ser? It will be, using ser:

turns.speaker: tutor
turns.text: Good. Sound familiar from anywhere?

turns.speaker: tutor
turns.text: Yes. So very simple, the future tense. Doesn’t matter if we have – ar, - er, or – ir verb. We are adding on these endings and these endings are the same as the different versions of haber. So if we forget them we can look back to haber and then we just add that on the end. But, in saying that, the future tense is not so often used in Spanish. Most of the time you will find yourself using present or voy a, vamos a. But the future tense does exist and now you know it.
